title: Church of St Benedict, Ardwick

infobox.value: [Ardwick](/wiki/Ardwick "Ardwick"), Manchester
England
[Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")
[Parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church")
[St Benedict](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia")
[Redundant](/wiki/Redundant_church "Redundant church")
[Grade II\* listed](/wiki/Listed_building#Categories_of_listed_building "Listed building")
26 February 1966
[J. S. Crowther](/wiki/J._S._Crowther "J. S. Crowther")
[Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival "Gothic Revival")
Brick

is a [redundant church](/wiki/Redundant_church "Redundant church") in the [Ardwick](/wiki/Ardwick "Ardwick") district of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England. The church is dedicated to the saint [Benedict of Nursia](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia"), was designed by [J. S. Crowther](/wiki/J._S._Crowther "J. S. Crowther") for a Manchester merchant John Marsland Bennett, and built in 1880. It is generally considered to be Crowther's masterpiece and is a [Grade II\* listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"). Declared redundant in the early 2000s, the church was home to Parthian Climbing Manchester, formerly the Manchester Climbing Centre. Parthian Climbing Manchester closed permanently in July 2025.

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") Heritage Record for St Benedict's incorrectly records the church as being [unlisted](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building").[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7)
John Dunne features in the 1998 British rock-climbing film, *[Hard Grit](/wiki/Hard_Grit "Hard Grit")*.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

The height of the nave was the principal attraction to John Dunne, who had seen climbing centres constructed in other redundant churches in England but none which offered the scale and size of St Benedict's.[[9]](#cite_note-auto2-10)

The **Church of St Benedict** is a [redundant church](/wiki/Redundant_church "Redundant church") in the [Ardwick](/wiki/Ardwick "Ardwick") district of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England. The church is dedicated to the saint [Benedict of Nursia](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia"), was designed by [J. S. Crowther](/wiki/J._S._Crowther "J. S. Crowther") for a Manchester merchant John Marsland Bennett, and built in 1880. It is generally considered to be Crowther's masterpiece and is a [Grade II\* listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"). Declared redundant in the early 2000s, the church was home to Parthian Climbing Manchester, formerly the Manchester Climbing Centre. Parthian Climbing Manchester closed permanently in July 2025.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_St_Benedict,_Ardwick&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

John Marsland Bennett (1817–1889) was a prosperous stone merchant who served two terms as [Mayor of Manchester](/wiki/List_of_lord_mayors_of_Manchester "List of lord mayors of Manchester") between 1863 and 1865. He lived at [Buile Hall](/wiki/Buile_Hill_Park "Buile Hill Park") in [Salford](/wiki/Salford "Salford").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Approached to donate land for a church to serve the growing community of Ardwick, he agreed both to provide the site and fund the building of the church.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartwellHydePevsner2004356–359-3) He also selected J. S. Crowther as architect, acted as project manager and took a keen interest in the church [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartwell2001294–297-4) Construction began in 1877 and the church was complete by 1880. In addition to the church, Crowther built an attached [clergy house](/wiki/Clergy_house "Clergy house") and [Sunday School](/wiki/Sunday_School "Sunday School").[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-5) The work cost Bennett £20,000.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

St Benedict's followed the [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholicism "Anglo-Catholicism") [High Church](/wiki/High_Church "High Church") tradition, offering masses rather than services. Falling attendance in the 20th century saw the church declared [redundant](/wiki/Redundant_church "Redundant church")[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7) and it closed in 2002.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[a]](#cite_note-9) In 2005, the church building reopened as the Manchester Climbing Centre. Its potential as an [indoor climbing](/wiki/Climbing_wall#Indoor_climbing "Climbing wall") centre was identified by a British [climber](/wiki/Rock_climbing "Rock climbing"), John Dunne, who had been looking for a suitable building in which to establish a club in [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England").[[9]](#cite_note-auto2-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[b]](#cite_note-13) The centre has one of the largest climbing walls in Europe.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

## Architecture and description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_St_Benedict,_Ardwick&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Architecture and description")]

Bennett's commission to Crowther emphasised that he wanted a church "plain but massive...[with] a shell which would be standing years after many cheap, 'dressy' churches had crumbled to ruins".[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartwellHydePevsner2004356–359-3) Crowther obliged; St Benedict's is "remarkably large", built of red [brick](/wiki/Brick "Brick") with [stone](/wiki/Ashlar "Ashlar") and [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") dressings.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartwell2001294–297-4) The style is [Early English Gothic](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture#Early_English_Gothic_(late_12th–late_13th_centuries) "English Gothic architecture"),[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-5) Crowther himself dating it, rather precisely, as "Early Geometric Decorated of the year 1245".[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartwell2001294–297-4) The [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") is very high, with a [hammerbeam roof](/wiki/Hammerbeam_roof "Hammerbeam roof") and ending in a tall tower.[[c]](#cite_note-15) The clergy house and Sunday school buildings are attached to the north side.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartwellHydePevsner2004356–359-3) St Benedict's has been a [Grade II\* listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") since 3 October 1974, its [Historic England](/wiki/Historic_England "Historic England") listing record describing it as "the most original of JS Crowther's church designs."[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-5)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_St_Benedict,_Ardwick&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") Heritage Record for St Benedict's incorrectly records the church as being [unlisted](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building").[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** John Dunne features in the 1998 British rock-climbing film, *[Hard Grit](/wiki/Hard_Grit "Hard Grit")*.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** The height of the nave was the principal attraction to John Dunne, who had seen climbing centres constructed in other redundant churches in England but none which offered the scale and size of St Benedict's.[[9]](#cite_note-auto2-10)
